title: The Impaired Colleague: Duty to Report and Patient Safety
subtitle: A nurse notices a coworker with slurred speech and an unsteady gait, frequent narcotic-count discrepancies, and patients reporting unrelieved pain. Patient safety comes first: remove the nurse from care, report up the chain, and support recovery — never cover it up.

patient.name: Impaired Colleague
patient.admitDate: A nurse suspects a coworker is impaired/diverting on shift: slurred speech, unsteady gait, count discrepancies, excessive PRN waste, and patients with unrelieved pain. The nurse must act to protect patients.

labs.v: Remove the nurse from patient care.
Per facility policy + chain of command.
Don't confront in front of patients.

nursesNote: A nurse notices that a coworker has slurred speech, an unsteady gait, and frequent unexplained absences from the unit, along with repeated narcotic-count discrepancies, excessive PRN waste and overrides, and several patients reporting unrelieved pain. These are warning signs of possible impairment and drug diversion. Patient safety is the first priority: the impaired nurse must be removed from patient care. The nurse reports the concern to the charge nurse or supervisor through the chain of command per facility policy, documents specific objective observations factually, and does not cover for the colleague, ignore the signs, or confront and accuse them publicly. The colleague's safety is also protected (not driving impaired). Reporting to the Board of Nursing may be required by state law. Substance use disorder is a treatable disease, and many states offer nondisciplinary peer-assistance or alternative-to-discipline recovery programs.
providerOrders: Ensure patient safety; relieve the impaired nurse of patient-care duties
Report the concern to the supervisor through the chain of command
Follow facility policy for evaluation and safe transport
Report to the Board of Nursing as required; support recovery referral

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What may indicate an impaired colleague?

opts.text: Frequent narcotic-count discrepancies and excessive PRN waste or overrides
Smell of alcohol, slurred speech, or an unsteady gait at work
Patients on the nurse's assignment frequently report unrelieved pain
Taking a scheduled coffee break is a sign of impairment
Frequent unexplained absences from the unit and mood changes
Repeatedly volunteering to administer all the unit's controlled medications
Patient safety comes first; an impaired nurse should be removed from patient care
Suspected impairment/diversion must be reported per policy and chain of command
Covering for the colleague or saying nothing is acceptable to protect them
Objective observations should be documented factually
Substance use disorder is a treatable disease; peer-assistance programs exist
The nurse should publicly confront and accuse the colleague on the unit

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response when you suspect impairment on shift

opts.text: Ensure patient safety — relieve the nurse of patient-care duties
Report the concern to the charge nurse/supervisor per policy
Document objective observations factually
Ensure the colleague's safety (not driving impaired) and arrange evaluation
Support referral to a peer-assistance/recovery program per policy
Removing the impaired nurse from patient care
Reporting suspicions up the chain of command
Documenting specific, objective observations
Ignoring the signs because the nurse is a friend
Letting the impaired nurse drive home alone
Helping the colleague hide narcotic-count discrepancies
Supporting referral to a treatment/recovery program
Confronting and accusing the nurse loudly in front of patients

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Relieve the nurse of duty and ensure patients are covered
Notify the nurse manager/supervisor immediately
Provide factual documentation of the observations
Follow facility policy for evaluation and safe transport
Report to the Board of Nursing as required and support recovery referral
Prioritizing patient safety by relieving the nurse of duty
Notifying the supervisor and documenting the facts
Treating substance use disorder as a treatable illness and supporting recovery
Reporting to the Board of Nursing as state law requires
Confronting the nurse alone and sending them home without reporting
Covering shifts and discrepancies so no one finds out
Retaliating against or gossiping about the colleague
Continuing to let the nurse administer controlled medications
